title: Why Is My Cat Licking Concrete or Walls? Causes and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat licking concrete? Learn about pica, anemia, and when this behavior requires a vet visit. Urgent care guide for cat owners.
content_html:
TL;DR: Licking concrete or walls is a behavior known as pica that can indicate underlying medical issues like feline anemia or nutritional deficiencies. While not an immediate emergency, seek veterinary care within 24 hours if your cat also shows signs of lethargy or pale gums.

Why is my cat licking non-food items like concrete, bricks, or walls?

  • This behavior is generally referred to as a form of pica, involving an appetite for substances that are largely non-nutritive.
  • Cats may be drawn to the cool temperature, the unique texture, or the trace minerals found in these materials.
  • The behavior is often an outward sign of an internal physiological need or a behavioral response to stress.

Is it an emergency if my cat is licking concrete or walls?

  • The urgency level for this behavior is Medium; while rarely an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires medical attention.
  • It is often a clinical sign of underlying issues such as feline anemia, nutritional deficiencies, or chronic kidney disease.
  • You should contact a veterinarian within 24 hours if your cat also exhibits pale gums, extreme lethargy, or is refusing their regular food.

How does sharing a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my cat’s behavior?

  • A video allows the veterinary team to see if the licking is compulsive or if it is triggered by specific environmental factors.
  • A clear photo of your cat's mouth and gums helps the triage professional check for signs of anemia, such as pale or white gums.
  • Visual evidence serves as a vital tool in identifying the specific type of pica and its potential causes.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Cats that lick or ingest non-nutritive substances such as concrete or walls may be exhibiting pica, a form of compulsive disorder. Medical problems must be excluded as a cause, as self-mutilation, excessive grooming, or self-directed aggression can stem from conditions causing neuropathic pain or pruritus, such as adverse food reactions, atopic dermatitis, and parasitic hypersensitivity. Neuropathic pain, dermatologic conditions, myopathies, and focal seizures can also manifest with similar signs; therefore, therapeutic trials for neuropathic pain, seizures, pruritus, or compulsive disorders may be part of the diagnostic process.

Chapter: Behavioral Medicine, Dermatology, Neurology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1574)

faqs.question: Can anemia cause a cat to lick walls?
faqs.answer: Yes, anemia is one of the most common medical reasons for this behavior. Cats with low red blood cell counts may instinctively seek out minerals like iron or calcium from concrete or dust.

faqs.question: Is licking concrete dangerous for my cat?
faqs.answer: While the concrete itself isn't typically toxic, licking it can lead to the ingestion of harmful bacteria, cleaning chemicals, or debris. It can also cause wear and tear on the tongue over time.

faqs.question: Could this be a sign of boredom?
faqs.answer: Yes, pica can sometimes be behavioral. If a cat is under-stimulated or anxious, they may develop repetitive licking habits as a way to self-soothe or pass the time.
